I totally understand that Ericsson didn't want hypnosis to be identified with that kind of thing. So I completely understand his effort in demystifying all that, but it's a disservice to the fact that that research has to go on because there is so much that is unexplained and I've had personal experiences myself that not in the sense that I am like a fortune teller or like an ESP person but that I have been told things that there was no way that the person could know. So I think we have to have respect for that research and it has to continue. And the other thing is that today some of the interventions with his own children could be considered borderline child abuse. In those days they were not. For example, when he lives in bed with his daughter Christie grabbing her by the ankle and it takes a long time for her to free herself, that's not anything you would recommend to any parent today when we have different insights about these things and how to deal with children. So I just want to point out that we can't have a blank acceptance of everything that Ericsson did. Now some of the stuff in the book is just totally remarkable. For example, the woman that had gone to various doctors and who laughed every time they examined her right breast and caused the doctor to stop and she had a malignant tumor in her right breast. ## [09:09 - 12:14]

An anchoring effect works like this and they tested it with research and they asked a group of people and they said at what age did Muhammad Gandhi die and they said 35 or 113. Now most people know that both of those are wrong because he had gray hair when he died and he looked elderly but they know also that he was killed and so he didn't live to 113. So they know that it was wrong so they would estimate, they would change the, they would just put in a number themselves, a third number. They asked another group what did Muhammad Gandhi die and they said 35 or 75 and what happens is that the people who were given the first option guessed that he died 15 years older than the other group and it was across all levels of types of people and education level and the reason is that when someone puts in a precedent like an age like that, yeah you're planning to think about it. It becomes your departure, your point of departure and then you deviate from it until you're not uncertain then you're not certain about it. So you're like it must be less than 113, maybe it's down and then at some point you don't know how much younger he is and that ends up being your answer and it's like that for everybody.
